data.title: Cloudflare: Avoid Stablecoin Hype
data.description:
Cloudflare, Inc. (NET) has made another run on AI-related hype. The AI connectivity cloud company continues to produce impressive results, but the stock is already priced for beyond perfection. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock, with the latest bump from stablecoins unrealistic.

Stablecoin Push

Last year, Cloudflare introduced the NET Dollar stablecoin concept for agentic commerce. The theory is that the stablecoin will be utilized by autonomous agents and connected devices to create a medium of exchange for automated transactions.

The Information reported last week that Cloudflare was working with Coinbase (COIN) or Zerohash to launch the stablecoin. The big question is why AI agents would want to use a random stablecoin from an AI infrastructure firm like Cloudflare versus just using the leading stablecoin from a fintech like USDC directly from Coinbase.

At the recent Morgan Stanley Technology Conference, CFO Thomas Seifert discussed the intent of the stablecoin offering as follows:

How do we sit between frontier models and content and give both sides the opportunity to monetize. There will be agentic AI workflows that do business on each other's behalf or on your behalf with micro transactions. So how do we build rails in order to facilitate that. That's why you hear us talking and partnering with pretty much every large payment provider, whether it's the credit card companies, whether it's PayPal or Coinbase. We talked about putting our own stablecoin in place in order to facilitate that. How that is going to play out in detail, I think there are a lot of questions still that need to be answered, but we are making really good progress building an ecosystem and building the rails that need to enable that.

In essence, Cloudflare is still exploring how to monetize the opportunity, so investors need to be careful paying up for the stock based on this concept. Stablecoins have huge potential, but outside of the USDC controlled by Coinbase and USDT controlled by Tether, the market is highly fragmented with a lot of smaller players.

Impressive Guidance Isn't Enough

Cloudflare reported Q4'25 earnings back on February 10. The company provided 2026 guidance based on a hot Agentic AI market, with discussion on OpenClaw and Claude Cowork on the earnings call and management pointing out that this work is typically done on Cloudflare Workers, so a lot of the recent excitement around AI agents should've been captured in guidance.

The key here is that management only guided to 2026 revenue growth in the 28.5% range. Cloudflare just ended 2025 at nearly 34%, so the guidance suggests the agentic AI boom isn't going to drive crazy growth.

Cloudflare definitely has a recent strong history of beating quarterly guidance by $5 to $10 million, so investors should plan on beats going forward. The original guidance was for 2025 revenues of up to $2.1 billion, and the company ended up at $2.17 billion for solid upside, but nothing gangbusters like hot AI companies.

Not only does Cloudflare trade at a rich multiple of nearly 200x 2026 EPS targets of $1.13, but the company isn't modeling much higher operating margins in the future. Cloudflare reported 2025 operating margins of 14%, and the long-term model is only 20%+.

Based on fully maximized targets, Cloudflare would produce an EPS in the range of $1.70 based on the 2026 revenue estimates of $2.8 billion and roughly 50% higher operating margin. The stock would still be extremely expensive based on maximized margin targets while the company still has to prove out the ability to reach those goals.

Cloudflare has a lot of promising growth ahead, but the market is still looking at a stock with 30% growth ending up at the end of the decade with a $3+ EPS target and still trading at 75x EPS. Unless the company figures out a way to fully monetize AI agents without giving up most of the profits to a fintech like Coinbase, the stock has no business trading above $200 for the rest of the decade.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Cloudflare is a great company benefitting from AI, but the AI connectivity business doesn't have AI-type growth like neoclouds and chip companies. The stock has such a massive premium valuation that total returns over the next several years are likely limited, and investors could face an extended period where Cloudflare trades lower.

data.title: Cincinnati Financial: A Safe Haven Amid Private Credit Fears
data.description:
Shares of Cincinnati Financial (CINF) have been a moderate performer over the past year, gaining about 10%. After some challenges, the company has made steady progress returning to a stronger level of core underwriting results, though concerns still linger about a broader compression in industry margins. CINF also has a unique investment portfolio, and while the insurance sector is generally noncyclical, this aspect of CINF’s financial position does impact its own cyclicality. Shares are up slightly since I last covered them in October, making my “Hold” rating appropriate. With updated financials and a revised macro outlook, now is a good time to revisit CINF.

In the company’s fourth quarter, reported on Feb. 9, Cincinnati Financial earned $3.37, which beat estimates by $0.48. Now, we have seen insurance stocks diverge meaningfully in recent months, irrespective of Q4 financial results, based on the perceived risk inside investment portfolios. Private credit has been a growing area of concern, but CINF is well-positioned to weather any challenges here.

CINF's Focus on Low-Risk Bonds and Stocks is a Net Positive

Cincinnati has a relatively unique investment portfolio with a primary allocation to high-quality public fixed income and then a secondary allocation to public equities. Investment income was up 9% from last year to $253 million. In total, the company has a $33.1 billion investment portfolio (including cash). Within this, it has an $18.2 billion fixed maturity profile; that portfolio yields 4.92%, which is essentially stable over the past year.

While many insurers have investment portfolios clouded in opacity, CINF details every single position it holds. Its portfolio is primarily composed of investment-grade corporate debt, municipal bonds, US treasuries, and just about $1 billion of asset-backed securities. It does not have the large direct lending or private credit exposure that has become a concern elsewhere in the insurance sector. That has helped shares hold in relatively well, and it remains a positive differentiator for the company.

To enhance returns, it still has a return-seeking portfolio, and CINF also has about $12.7 billion in equities. The cost basis of this portfolio is about $4.2 billion, so this allocation has been a meaningful driver of long-term value creation. Obviously, equity prices can move meaningfully with economic activity and sentiment, so its portfolio still has some clear cyclicality in it. However, in this environment, exposure to public equities provides greater clarity and is easier to value than a large private credit holding. The construction of its overall portfolio is similar to a company like Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) with low-risk fixed income and an equity allocation. While CINF is a long-term holder of stocks, it has a diverse portfolio that is more index-like in nature.

Solid Underwriting, but Margin Expansion Potential is Limited

Turning next to the company’s insurance operation, underwriting results were solid in the fourth quarter as the company generated an 85.2% combined ratio in its property casualty segment, lowering the full-year ratio to 94.9%. Its Q4 combined ratio was up 50 bps from last year, but this remains a very strong level. For the full year, its combined ratio was up 150 bps, but this was primarily due to the catastrophe environment. Its current year combined ratio before cats was actually 40 bps better. CINF had significant losses from the California wildfires, which created a difficult first half of the year. Overall, though, it was a fairly average cat year, and I view its underwriting results as consistent with my ~95% long-term combined ratio assumption.

Across all lines excluding workers compensation, management expects premium inflation to exceed loss trends, leading to a stronger margin environment this year. The workers comp market has faced elevated competition, limiting pricing power, creating some headwinds for 2026. Overall, pricing has grown more competitive, and so I expect any margin expansion to be modest in nature. Indeed, my base case is for flat overall underwriting margins.

Interestingly, new business agency premiums were 13% lower in Q4. This decline in new activity coincided with a more competitive pricing environment, and to an extent, I believe this more competitive environment has led CINF to pull back from some policies where it questions the attractiveness of the potential margin. Ultimately, I prefer to see insurers protect margins than chase growth and risk writing unprofitable business.

During its annual reinsurance negotiations, it was able to increase its reinsurance coverage by about 11% to $2 billion while increasing costs by just about 7%, or $12 million. This speaks to some of the pricing deflation we have seen in reinsurance markets as capital has returned after a quiet hurricane season. This improved pricing should support CINF’s own margins and help to mitigate any slowing of its own premium pricing to customers.

Commercial Leads: Further Personal Line Improvement is Needed

Looking at segment results, commercial new premiums grew 7% to $1.24 billion, and CINF generated an 88.4% combined ratio. This was up 390 bps worse than last year, driving a 20% drop in underwriting profits to $144 million. This increase was entirely driven by higher non-cat losses in 2025. Commercial auto pricing was up mid-single-digits. This has been an area of concern given persistently high claims, and at this level of pricing, I believe CINF can sustain margins in 2026.

Its personal line business remains a comparative laggard as the company has been trying to build scale and better establish its brand. This has led to solid premium growth of 18% in Q4, but the unit remains loss-making with a full-year 103.6% combined ratio. As the company continues to grow, unit economics should improve, and it is critical we see progress in 2026 towards generating an underwriting profit. Importantly, its ex-cat combined ratio improved 130 bps in 2025 to 81.6%. That said, given the highly commoditized nature of many personal line businesses, I expect it to have a structurally higher combined ratio than its commercial business, likely in the 96-99% zone.

Finally, its excess and surplus business grew 12% to $188 million. Margins remained strong with an 84.7% combined ratio, down 840 bps from last year. This was aided by lower cat losses and a reserve release. It also has a small life insurance unit, and net income grew by 11% to $31 million in Q4 thanks to better underwriting results and benefits from the higher rate environment.

Shares Are Fairly Valued and Are a Suitable Hold

Shares offer a 2.4% dividend yield. CINF has increased its dividend for 65 straight years, and I do not see that streak being broken anytime soon. Thanks to buybacks, its share count was down about 0.4% from last year. I expect its dividend to remain the primary source of capital return.

When valuing CINF, I like to value it on a sum-of-the-parts basis, valuing the core insurance business and then adding in the value of its equities. Given the decline in stocks recently, its portfolio is likely worth ~$12.4 billion, or about $78. Using a 95% combined ratio, its insurance operations (and net investment income) should earn about $7.00-$7.20, or about $85 at 12x earnings. This assumes ~4% premium growth, a continued slowing from 2025 given more limited pricing opportunities.

All in, I see CINF being worth about $165, similar to my prior view. This represents about 4% upside, or a ~7% total return perspective. As such, I view shares as a reasonable "Hold," especially for investors concerned about private credit, as its portfolio construction should help to insulate the company from potential volatility here.

data.title: GDX: Gold Miners, Royalty And Exploration; Searching For A Low
data.description:
At time of posting, President Trump has backed off of an ultimatum to Iran. This came after gold mining stocks (GDX) and the metals had tanked in pre-market on Monday, registering the lower of our projected levels.

The gold mining stock correction is playing out mostly as expected, now hitting targets

Back in January we targeted the PDAC (mining trade show) time frame (1st week of March) for a much needed correction of the excesses to begin. Gold stocks, along with gold and silver were due for a beat down after all that 2025 bullishness and market leadership.

This was aside from the fundamentals, which have deteriorated (for mining operations) to a moderate degree in 2026. It was simply market mechanics. The market was going to find some excuse to knock gold stocks down. That excuse finally came in the form of war and the resulting spike in oil prices (oil/energy being a heavy factor in mining costs).

It may sound obvious, but there is a distinction within the gold stock universe. Mining companies will be at least temporarily dragged by the spike in energy. Royalty and Exploration generally will not. These are sector fundamentals. On the macro, the wider gold stock correction does not care about sector fundamentals. It cares about emotion, and there’s plenty of that flying around right now.

The Bullish Percent Index BPGDM is on a “buy” signal, as it is oversold to the degree of the 2015 low, which launched the bull market. But you will notice a broken trend in the indicator. Gold stocks are going to bounce, rally or potentially incinerate the shorts. But the broken bull trend in BPGDM implies a primary correction. Per yesterday’s in-week subscriber notes:

Note 4 was discussion of the BPGDM and its implications. Notes 1-3 included gold stocks I bought on Thursday (under cover of gold miner, gold and silver short hedges) as they tanked to downside targets we’d projected in last weekend's report.

Fundamental Note — Mining vs. Royalty and Exploration

As far back as last year my view was that more commodities — including energy commodities — would join the bull market. Royalty companies (e.g. Royal Gold, Wheaton, Franco and the likes of several smaller and intermediate Royalties) and Mineral Exploration operations (hole drillers) would have more weight in our view. That is because their cost structures will be relatively unscathed by the effects of inflation. Unlike actual miners, which will see their cost structures compromised with rising energy inputs.

GDX and the Daily Chart Technical Situation

You noticed no bullish public posts from me during 2026 so far, and from a post on January 25 discussing corrective options for the NYSE Arca Gold BUGS Index, HUI:

…the first correction could feel like a cyclical bear market in its intensity.

As to the gold stock (miners, royalty and exploration) correction in general, it turned out not to be as simple as picking a target. Our favored targets are highlighted at left of each of the Fibonacci grids shown on the daily chart of GDX below. The upper levels were hit on Thursday at a clear visual support (green shaded) in the 80-82 area.

But over the scope of the correction, it was not as easy as picking a downside target and preparing for it. Because of course GDX/HUI, etc. put in marginal higher highs (double top) amid negative RSI divergence at point 'B' of an A-B-C corrective structure. The volume into corrective leg 'A' looks like it was climactic, but it was not. It was the first leg getting kicked out from under the table. Then a rise to 'B' on low volume into a cruel bull trap. Then?

Here we are. The gold stock correction has made itself known to one and all after we tracked the process for all of 2026 so far. We were well prepared for the now hysterical downside event taking place (personally, short to hedge as noted above). Note that all hedges were profitably covered due to the now very positive risk/reward situation, at the time of writing.

While there could easily be more short-term downside, as per the clip of Thursday’s note above, the gold stock sector is in a much better risk/reward situation. A majority of the downside is in the books and it is time to slowly, patiently plot buying opportunities for a tradeable rally, at least.

Beyond Gold Stocks

As noted above, whatever unfolds, the gold stock sector is no longer special going forward, as wider inflation trades are expected to take hold across commodities and certain markets. Oil and the current inflation mini-hysteria is not that. Not yet. Note that spiking oil prices are not the result of monetary policy, hence not the result of inflation. The last inflationary operation took place in H1, 2020 as the Fed dropped rates to zero, and the government (President Trump's first administration, then President Biden's) implemented various manners of financial stimulus, including sending checks to individuals. The inflation problem then predictably became apparent starting in 2021. When oil prices resolve, I expect disinflation to continue and likely morph to a deflation scare prior to the next inflationary operation, which will at first feel good, but soon turn quite unpleasant in its after-effects.

I expect 2027 to ultimately shake out inflationary. But in the interim we continue to await the first deflation scare of the new macro. Once again, our pictorial view of the new macro:

The bottom line is that due to the effects of a new inflation problem to come and increasing global trade tensions, more commodities are expected to participate in the bull, after gold miners led the process in 2025. Within the gold stock universe, royalty and exploration companies will be less vulnerable to the corrosive effects of inflation.

data.title: New Fortress Energy: Existing Shareholders Draw Short Straw Under U.K. Restructuring Plan
data.description:
New Fortress Energy Inc. (NASDAQ:NFE) announced a substantial Restructuring Support Agreement that will vary creditor claims with a debt-for-equity swap, subsequently deleveraging its balance sheet through a Restructuring Plan (RP) under part 26A of the Companies Act of 2006.

This article will consider the background of the Restructuring Plan, its recent struggles, and the prospects for existing shareholders in the 'New NFE' post-restructuring.

So, What Is The Restructuring Plan?

Whilst the NFE restructuring may seem to be a 'done deal' by the third quarter of this year, it is by no means so. The UK courts must sanction the deal. This won't necessarily be a smooth task; the Restructuring Plan as a comprehensive restructuring tool has already faced a bumpy road.

Background - A Powerful Tool

First, let's bring in some background information around the Restructuring Plan. It was introduced in June 2020, very quickly after the onset of the pandemic, as part of the UK's Company Insolvency and Governance Act.

The Restructuring Plan promised to bring a new comprehensive tool to the kit of distressed businesses in that it allowed a cross-cramdown of creditors. A Restructuring Plan can be approved even if the required number of votes in favor of the plan are not met. This is to prevent a 'roadblock,' where certain dissenting creditors prevent the company from being rescued as a going concern.

Difficulties Since Implementation

'Are Restructuring Plans losing their appeal'? This was the pressing question in the UK insolvency industry in 2025. In fact, a large share of 2025 UK restructuring reviews written by law firms highlighted the challenging year that the Restructuring Plan faced. Take Freshfields and DLA Piper as examples.

Before this, the threshold for approval was deemed to be quite low, with the courts demonstrating a willingness to cram down creditors across a number of different cases.

The change in tune in 2025 was down to two landmark court decisions:

  1. Waldorf production - the English court refused to sanction the restructuring plan due to unfair allocation of the benefits of the plan between different classes of creditors.
  2. Petrofac - The Court of Appeal overturned the high court-sanctioned plan. This was on the ground of 'fairness.'

In both cases the court sided in favor of the dissenting 'out of money' creditors. The core focus for the court was that the companies failed to demonstrate that the allocation of benefits in the plans was fair. For Waldorf, this came because they failed to adequately engage with unsecured creditors. For Petrofac, they could not demonstrate that the terms the plan offered were better terms than they would have obtained if they went and tested the market.

New Fortress Will Provide Another Test

As the Restructuring Plan is a relatively new option, it continues to evolve through case law in the UK, and interpretation is shaped by new cases that work through the court.

NFE's plan will be the biggest test for the Restructuring Plan's effectiveness to date. As a bare minimum, New Fortress will need to meet the thresholds of fair allocation of value and demonstrate that those not supporting the plan would be no worse off under the plan than the alternative - in this case, that would be Chapter 11 bankruptcy in the US. Under Chapter 11, equity holders are typically wiped out before debt is compromised.

Hence we see the following quote from New Fortress' CFO when discussing the agreement on the conference call:

The liability subject to compromise was critical as we agreed the U.K. process preserved significantly greater value, but also gave us some of the benefits that can be achieved in the Chapter 11 process.

CEO Wes Edens said that NFE has support from more than 50% of bond and loan holders, expecting this to exceed the 75% threshold by completion.

Considering New Fortress has strong support across creditor classes (most importantly including certain unsecured creditors) and preserves more value for shareholders and creditors than an insolvent procedure, the plan is likely to be approved and seen as 'just and equitable.'

Position For Shareholders

The agreement would cut NFE's debt pile from $5.7b to $527.5m. But this involves 'cramming down' shareholders significantly under the plan.

If the plan is approved by the UK courts, then shareholders are set to be diluted significantly. Existing common shareholders will immediately lose control of the Brazilian LNG assets under the plan, as this will be 'separated' and handed over to creditors.

Shareholders will retain an interest in the newco (ex-Brazil assets), though this will be diluted down to just 35% of the common equity. To compound the pain here for shareholders, an extra $2.5b of preferred equity will be given to creditors. This can be converted to 87% of equity if not redeemed within three years.

The market seemed to welcome the non-Chapter 11 outcome initially with a share price jump of 28.4%. However, these gains were gone within the following few hours, and shares now trade down.

Pumps in distressed businesses stock prices have become quite common following restructuring announcements in recent times, however the reality of these terms for common shares clearly proved too harsh to ignore.

Should You Buy New Fortress Shares?

No. The value left on the table for common shareholders is minimal, and I'd argue that the prospect of future dilution from preferred equity is significant. While the Brazil arm spinoff means a huge loss of value.

The former debt structure with a cost of debt exceeding 10% and the new preferred equity accruing dividends at 3% in the first year before ramping up in years 2 and 3:

The new NFE contains a mixture of 3 LNG terminals, power plants, a turbine portfolio, and a liquefier.

It also seems that the management team is more focused on the three growth initiatives detailed below, which will incur significant reinvestment and capex and are execution-dependent initiatives.

Management anticipates the Nicaragua terminal will be commissioned by October 2026 of this year, however this could still be subject to delays.

This also then makes it less likely the preferred equity will be paid down prior to conversion over a short three-year timeframe upon execution of the Restructuring Plan. Unlevered free cash flow is expected to total just $680m from FY27 through FY29, which provides limited liquidity to repay preferred equity.

NFE has also had troublesome financial projections in the past few years. Therefore the 'new NFE' projected $400m EBITDA in FY27 should be taken with a pinch of salt. Under these forecasts, EBITDA is expected to almost triple from the current base platform with incremental gains from the previously mentioned initiatives.

In essence, c.60+% of NFE's FY27 EBITDA is the growth initiatives; this underpins the heightened uncertainty highlighted above.

On the conference call, the CFO highlighted that the FY27 forecasts would provide a multiple of 10x EV/EBITDA based. This is now slightly lower after the subsequent decline in stock price (around 9x).

I think the decline is due to a mixture of uncertainty on the execution of the new initiatives, considering historic misses that contributed to NFE's current position, and regauging the likelihood of substantial conversion of the preferred equity balance.

The Bottom Line

In many ways, NFE is the model distressed situation to use the UK Restructuring Plan. Management is clearly excited by the plan, and rightly so; it significantly deleverages the business while avoiding disruptions in NFE's operation. It's clearly better than the alternative scenario, where NFE will point the court to the significantly larger losses for the majority of creditors under Chapter 11.

Shareholders, however, need to consider the current value of equity in the context of future dilution and the implications that has on the Enterprise Value. The market has, for a number of months, even following Bloomberg's article rumoring the restructuring, priced shares with the consideration that existing equity holders would receive an interest in the new business.

It did not, however, foresee the level of dilution that common shareholders were set to experience, and we are now seeing the market adjust to this, with consecutive daily declines. Whilst shares may experience a bump/short squeeze in the near term (pre-deal conclusion), I believe the existing equity value sits lower over the medium term and therefore rate shares a 'sell.'

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Insmed: "Strong Buy" As Possible Label Expansion For ARIKAYCE Bodes Well
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Insmed (INSM) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Insmed: Company Presses On With Further POC PAH Treatment Data." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was a "Strong Buy" based on the fact that it had achieved positive results from its phase 2b study using Treprostinil Palmitil Inhalation Powder [TPIP] for the treatment of patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension [PAH]. I'm going to keep this rating on this stock for this reason and for several other reasons that have occurred since then. In terms of the development of TPIP for the treatment of patients with hypertension, this program is coming along very well. One reason is because the company expects to initiate a phase 3 study using TPIP for the treatment of patients with PAH in the 1st half of 2026. The second reason is because the company has already expanded to another similar patient population. It is actively enrolling in the ongoing phase 3 PALM-ILD study using TPIP for the treatment of patients with pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease [PH-ILD]. This program in itself is extensive.

Consider that the company is looking to release data from its phase 2b open-label extension [OLE] trial using TPIP for the treatment of patients with PAH in the 2nd half of 2026. Along with it expecting to branch out to using this inhalation powder for evaluation in the targeting of patients with primary progressive fibrosis [PPF] and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF]. Both of such phase 3 study programs are expected to be initiated in the 2nd half of 2026. There was another win that happened since I last spoke about this company. It did indeed receive FDA approval of dipeptidyl peptidase 1 [DPP1] inhibitor BRINSUPRI [brensocatib] as the first and only non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis [NCFB] in adults and pediatric patients ages 12 and older. It also received European Commission [EC] approval of this drug for these patients in November of 2025.

With possible additional approvals for this expected in the United Kingdom [UK] and Japan in 2026. What I want to go over this time around would be that of the Accelerated Approval of ARIKAYCE for refractory Mycobacterium avium complex [MAC] lung disease. That's because, as I will be going over below, it just achieved positive results from its phase 3b ENCORE study using ARIKAYCE + multi-drug therapy for untreated MAC lung disease patients. In essence, it will be able to satisfy the FDA's requirement to convert Accelerated Approval to "Full" approval with this study for patients with refractory MAC lung disease, plus apply for a supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] to go after 1st-line patients. With all of the progress it had made to date, plus ARIKAYCE full-year 2025 revenues of $433.8 million [19% annual growth], I believe that this stock should remain with a "Strong Buy" rating.

Positive Results From Phase 3b ENCORE Study Accomplishes Two Tasks At The Same Time

As I noted above, Insmed has been able to do well in terms of the several programs it is advancing in its pipeline. Plus, the sales it has been able to and will be able to generate with ARIKAYCE and BRINSUPRI. What I want to go over for the purpose of this article is its recent release of its financials for Q4 and full-year 2025 and also the positive results it had just achieved in a late-stage clinical trial. In terms of its earnings, I will be going over this below in the "Financials" subsection of this article. What I want to go over now is the positive results it just obtained from its phase 3b ENCORE study using ARIKAYCE plus multi-drug therapy for untreated MAC Lung Disease. Before going over this entire program, plus any catalysts to come about from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

The thing is that Mycobacterium avium complex [MAC] lung infection is caused by a collection of bacteria known as Mycobacterium avium complex that is all around people. It can be found in the soil, water, and dust particles. The truth is that despite this being everywhere, a lot of people who are healthy are not affected by it. This type of bacterium tends to be found in people who either have a weakened immune system or who already have another chronic lung disease. Furthermore, MAC lung disease can be split into two different types:

  • Nodular bronchiectatic - formation of tiny knots in the airways or small air sacs of the lungs - leads to inflammation and then scarring of the lungs; The final outcome not being able to clear mucus and lead to cascading effect of other invading bacteria
  • Fibrocavitary disease - more severe form of MAC, that leads to holes in the lungs

One important item to note is that this disorder is not contagious; thus, it can't be passed from one person to another. Some symptoms that these patients might experience are:

  • Coughing up blood
  • Fatigue
  • Night sweats
  • Shortness of breath

The global Nontuberculous mycobacterial [NTM] lung disease market is expected to grow to $2.92 billion by 2030. This is a very large market for sure, and the company is now in even better shape than before. The reason why I state this is because ARIKAYCE was given Accelerated Approval for refractory MAC lung disease. The positive data I'm about to go over below might allow it to target the 200k 1st-line patients it couldn't before [Insmed Topline Results Phase 3b ENCORE Study Presentation March 23, 2026, slide #6]. That's because in terms of the refractory MAC lung disease patient population, there were only roughly around 30k possible.

The way that ARIKAYCE works is by delivering the aminoglycoside antibiotic amikacin via liposomes, which are better to enter biofilm-producing MAC. Once these liposomes enter into the body, the macrophages engulf them. Why is it important for this process to happen? That's because the MAC bacteria like to hide and reside in macrophages. The amikacin is then released internally to kill off bacteria by inhibiting protein synthesis. In order to see whether or not Insmed would be successful in being able to treat these patients with 1st-line MAC disease, it recruited a total of 425 adults with MAC lung disease in the phase 3b ENCORE study. The key thing here for this late-stage trial is that these patients have not received any antibiotics whatsoever before entering this trial. With that being said, these adults with MAC lung disease were randomized 1:1 to receive one of the following treatment regimens:

  • 213 patients given ARIKAYCE + azithromycin 250 mg + ethambutol 15 mg/kg
  • 212 patients given Placebo [inhaled empty liposome control] + azithromycin 250 mg + ethambutol 15 mg/kg

The patients received treatment for a total of 12 months. However, the primary endpoint for this study was evaluated at month 13 [one month after patients were off treatment]. The primary endpoint of this trial was to evaluate the change from baseline in the Respiratory Symptom Score [RSS] at month 13. How was this to be measured? It was analyzed using something known as Quality of Life – Bronchiectasis (QoL-B) Respiratory Domain [total of 8 domain items]. This is a patient-reported outcome that signifies how a person is coming along in terms of domains of respiratory symptoms, emotional perception, treatment burden, and other measures. The higher this score is, the better improvement a patient is experience. On the flip side, a decrease in score signifies that the patient is in worse shape. The final outcome was that patients who took the ARIKAYCE + multidrug arm of azithromycin + ethambutol achieved a greater increase change from baseline in terms of RSS at month 13 compared to that of the placebo. Patients on the ARIKAYCE treatment combination regimen achieved an increase of 17.77 points compared to the placebo arm with only 14.66 points. This ends up being a statistically significant point difference of 3.11 points with a p-value of p=0.0299. This is definitely a good outcome, and even though this primary endpoint was only established to evaluate patients at 13 months, the difference between the drug regimen and placebo one increased further with additional months. At month 15, it was noted that the treatment difference between the ARIKAYCE + multi-drug therapy combination and placebo was higher by 4.80 points [Insmed Topline Results Phase 3b ENCORE Study Presentation March 23, 2026, slide #10]. This proves that over time the drug still continued to work past the original primary endpoint time frame.

One milestone to consider is that the company expects to present these results from the phase 3b ENCORE study at an upcoming medical conference. However, the reason why I'm bullish is because the positive results obtained here give this company the freedom to accomplish several things. The first of which is that it is looking to submit a supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] of ARIKAYCE to cover 1st-line MAC lung disease patients in the 2nd half of 2026. The second thing is that while it attempts to expand its label to include 1st-line MAC lung disease patients, it is also going to attempt to obtain traditional approval for the use of this drug for the treatment of refractory MAC lung disease patients. Why is that? That's because the first time around, the FDA only granted Accelerated Approval of ARIKAYCE for refractory patients. This will give the company the opportunity to knock out two items at once. Of course, that's only if it is successful in doing so.

The third item that it can accomplish with the positive phase 3b ENCORE data is to discuss this data with the Pharmaceuticals and Medical Devices Agency [PMDA]. Should this go well, then the company could expand the label of ARIKAYCE for Japan as well for these MAC lung disease patients. Speaking of which, the hurdle for Japanese regulators was different. The primary endpoint to potentially expand the label in Japan for these patients was the proportion of patients who achieved both Culture Conversion at month 6 and durable Culture Conversion at month 15. The company easily met this threshold, as statistically significant greater conversion rates were observed at months 6, 12, 13, and 15 [Insmed Topline Results Phase 3b ENCORE Study Presentation March 23, 2026, slide #16].

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Insmed had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $1.4 billion as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this company is generating revenues each and every quarter. Things are looking bullish for both ARIKAYCE and BRINSUPRI. In terms of ARIKAYCE, this drug is seeing some very good growth annually. In the most recent Q4 of 2025 and Full-year 2025 revenue release report, it noted that full-year 2025 sales of ARIKACYE were $433.8 million. Why is this number important? That's because not only does this amount to a 19% year-over-year growth rate, but it exceeded the upper end of prior 2025 guidance. What I noted above is important, because with positive phase 3b ENCORE study data on hand, it is going to attempt to seek to expand its label to include 1st-line MAC lung disease patients as well. Where before it could only go after 30,000 refractory patients, it will be able to treat roughly around 200,000 + 1st-line patients.

BRINSUPRI is also coming along well as well, having been approved by the FDA for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis [NCFB] in adults and pediatric patients aged 12 and older on August 12, 2025. Along with the European Commission approving this drug for these patients 12 years of age and older with two or more exacerbations in the prior 12 months. The bullish nature of its earnings is the guidance it has given for BRINSUPRI, as it believes that this drug in 2026 could generate revenues of at least $1 billion. Even with the EPS miss it just had for its earnings, I believe it is bullish on the guidance it has given for BRINSUPRI. That, plus the fact that it still expects ARIKAYCE revenues to be between a range of $450 million and $470 million. The company burns about $583.5 million of cash per quarter. This is in terms of R&D expenses, SG&A expenses, cost of product revenues, and other expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Insmed. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the recently released positive results from the phase 3b ENCORE study using ARIKAYCE plus multi-drug therapy to treat 1st-line MAC lung disease patients. The hope is that the company is going to be able to file an sNDA of this drug to expand its label to target this 1st-line pool of patients in the 2nd half of 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that it will be able to expand its label to include this other batch of 200,000+ MAC lung disease patients. Nor that the company is going to be allowed to convert its Accelerated Approval to full traditional approval in the refractory MAC lung disease setting.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing evaluation of oral capsule DPP1 inhibitor brensocatib in the phase 2b CEDAR study for the treatment of patients with hidradenitis suppurativa [HS]. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this drug will end up working well for the treatment of these patients, nor that the primary endpoint of percent change from baseline in Total Abscess and Inflammatory Nodule [AN] count at Week 16 is going to be met in a statistically significant manner.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of treprostinil palmitil inhalation powder for the treatment of patients with PAH. The company is aiming to initiate a phase 3 study for this program in the 1st half of 2026. The risk here is that even though the company was able to achieve a statistically significant 35% placebo-adjusted reduction from baseline in Pulmonary Vascular Resistance [PVR] in a statistically significant manner, there is no assurance that this will be replicated again in the soon-to-be initiated phase 3 study. In addition, it remains to be seen what the established primary endpoint will end up being. That is, if the FDA allows PVR to be a primary endpoint, or the company is forced to tackle a different efficacy endpoint entirely.

Conclusion

Insmed is continuing to do well with the three products from its pipeline I went over above. The main item I went over was its potential to expand the label of ARIKAYCE to include the treatment of 1st-line MAC lung disease patients. This is going to be possible thanks to the positive results it achieved from the phase 3b ENCORE study, showing that ARIKAYCE, along with azithromycin plus ethambutol, was able to achieve an improvement in terms of RSS at 13 months compared to placebo. This ended up being a 17.77 point increase from baseline in RSS for the ARIKAYCE treatment regimen, compared to only 14.66 points for placebo.

At the same time, it is gunning to convert its Accelerated Approval of this drug in refractory MAC lung disease patients to traditional full approval as well. The potential for this company is endless, because the opportunity for ARIKAYCE alone is good enough. This doesn't even include the potential of TPIP being advanced for the treatment of patients with PAH, PH-ILD, IPF, and PPF. Lastly, BRINSUPRI for the treatment of non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis [NCFB] patients is still continuing to expand. For starters, regulatory decisions to expand this drug to the UK and Japan are expected in 2026.

Along with what I briefly mentioned above, which is that the DPP1 inhibitor bresnocatib is also potentially being developed for Immunology and Inflammatory [I & I] disorders as well. The pipeline shows its expansions towards the targeting of patients with HS, Rheumatoid Arthritis [RA], and inflammatory bowel disorders [IBDs]. With the ability to expand its label with the sNDA of ARIKAYCE for the treatment of 1st-line MAC lung disease patients, plus the potential development of several blockbusters in its pipeline, I believe that Insmed should remain with a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: 3 Asset Classes And 3 Industries Already In Bear Markets
data.description:
Equities staged a big rebound to start a new trading week on Monday. Albeit one that saw some profit-taking late in the day. Both the Dow and S&P gained an identical 1.38%, and the NASDAQ rose 1.15%, while the Russell 2000 climbed 2.29%. That was a complete bounce back from Friday's selloff in small caps when the Russell 2000 went into 'official' correction territory. The administration held off from hitting energy and infrastructure targets in Iran for now, while the POTUS stated he held 'productive talks' with leaders of Iran, which was denied by that counterparty. That pushed oil prices down by more than 9% on the day.

This feels more like a relief rally than anything concrete in my view. The endgame to the conflict with Iran remains unarticulated. Until details get meted out, I consider any pronouncements on this front to be not much more than Hopium.

With yesterday's rally, the S&P 500 is just under six percent from its recent highs despite huge rises in energy, natural gas, fertilizer, and other key commodity costs. The headline S&P 500 is hiding stress in much of the market. In today's piece I discuss three asset classes and three industries that are already in bear markets to highlight how complacent some investors have become by just monitoring key index levels.

Let's start with Bitcoin. The largest cryptocurrency in the world has fallen from just over $125,000 in early October to just over $70,000 currently. A decline of roughly 35%. Other cryptocurrencies like Ethereum have posted larger declines over this time.

There are many reasons for these steep declines. Bitcoin had staged a huge rise prior to its recent pullback, rising from under $27,000 in early October to just over $125,000 at its peak two years ago. Investor sentiment has also moved off riskier assets. Worries about higher inflation, additional regulatory scrutiny, and potentially tighter monetary policy in the offing have also been factors for the sell-off. Or at least no more cuts to the Fed Funds rate on the horizon. Approximately $2 trillion of value has been wiped off due to this sell-off in cryptocurrencies. About the size of the private credit markets. The narrative that Bitcoin is a 'store of value' also has taken a substantial hit due to the sharpness of this decline.

A more historical store of value, namely gold, is now in a bear market of its own despite a new regional war in the Middle East. The yellow metal hit its all-time high of $5,589.38 per ounce intraday on January 28th. Gold prices are down roughly 22% since. Like Bitcoin, the precious metal had a huge run in the two years prior to its recent decline. Higher yields make interest-bearing assets more attractive. This reduces demand for gold and other precious metals. Combined with silver, this market has seen a roughly $2 trillion decline in value from their highs earlier this year.

Small caps have suffered much worse than the headline S&P 500 index. With a decline of 2.26% on Friday, the Russell 2000 entered an official 10% 'correction' before rallying 2.29% on Monday, as previously noted. Morgan Stanley noted on March 17th that over 50% of the stocks in the Russell 3000 have had at least 20% declines from their recent highs. So, most of the index is in a bear market.

Turning to industries already in bear market territory, we look first at Airlines. The industry is getting hit on several fronts. The situation in Iran and the corresponding rise in oil prices have triggered a doubling of Jet fuel prices. One of the primary operating costs for airlines. This is causing companies like United Airlines (UAL) to cut flights, at least temporarily.

The other major problem the industry faces is due to the latest partial government shutdown: TSA agents have not been paid since February. Several hundred have already quit their positions. Many others have been calling out sick. This has resulted in very long lines at major airports like Atlanta Hartsfield and LaGuardia, which are generating considerable headlines, which isn't conducive to consumer travel. I mean, who wants to wait in line for 2-3 hours to board a flight if they don't have to?

And if business travel, which is much higher margin, falls off, events could become quite dire for the industry. It is hardly any wonder why the industry is in a bear market. American Airlines (AAL) is off over 25% year-to-date in 2026 even after a big rally on Monday thanks to the fall in oil prices. United is doing a bit better as its jet fuel costs are better hedged than American, and Delta is the standout in the industry as it owns its own refinery. Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV) is down nearly 30% from its highs in mid-February.

Not surprisingly, the miners are in a bear market. The State Street SPDR S&P Metals & Mining ETF (XME) is down over 25% from its recent highs on January 23rd. The sell-off is due to two primary factors. Obviously, the first is the recent falls in gold, silver, and copper prices. The second is the big rise in energy prices, which is a huge operational cost across the industry. If the situation resolves itself in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz reopens, I would expect a big rebound in this sector. I own covered call positions in miners like Hecla Mining Company (HL) and Newmont Corporation (NEM), it should be noted.

Investors should also be watching the financial sector, which is off to its worst start to a year since 2020. Specifically, the alternative investment space is getting shellacked. Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), with approximately $300 billion in AUM, is down over 55% from its 52-week high. Much larger Blackstone Inc. (BX) is off roughly 45% from its highs last October, and Ares Management Corporation (ARES) has fallen a similar percentage from its highs last August.

The point of this exercise is to highlight that while the major indexes are not even near correction territory, things are much worse under the surface. Many areas of the markets are not only in correction territory but are in bear markets. Some $4 trillion collectively has been lost in precious metals and cryptocurrencies from their recent highs.

In addition, the problems in the private credit markets continue to multiply. Apollo Global Management (APO) became the latest large firm to 'gate' investor redemption requests this week. On Monday, the firm disclosed they were only allowing 45% of redemption requests in a $15 billion private credit fund. Moody's also downgraded a private credit fund that is run by KKR & Co. (KKR) and Future Standard to junk.

In short, the recent 'dip' in equities should only be bought very incrementally. If the situation around the Strait of Hormuz does not get resolved in short order, significantly lower entry points could be in the offing.

data.title: SPYI Vs. QDPL: The More Reliable Income Strategy Across Cycles
data.description:
On paper, the methodology embedded in the Pacer Metaurus US Large Cap Dividend Multiplier 400 ETF (QDPL) looks very promising compared to covered call-based strategies. This is because it appears to allow undeterred upside capture in S&P 500 rallies that an option strategy restricts theoretically. QDPL is indeed a strong income play, but its thesis actually works out in narrow conditions - conditions we have seen in the few years since its inception. But QDPL does not retain its edge as an income plan over covered call plays when it comes to recessions, sharp drawdowns, or even consolidating markets. That makes it less reliable in market conditions I am bracing for in 2026, which indicates anything but a sharp rally.

This thesis dives into the mechanics of QDPL and compares its relative utility versus a popular covered call alternative, the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI). It finds QDPL a decent portfolio Hold, for market conditions ahead, but I would continue to rate SPYI as a Buy and position it as overweight within the income portfolio. SPYI has survived a comparison with a methodologically superior covered call play, GPIX, the last time I reviewed it. And it again survives a comparison with another apparently stronger play like QDPL. SPYI could actually be the ETF to own in markets that are unlikely to need upside capture like it has in the past few years of bull market conditions. Even when it comes to that, the perceived gap does not really show up as much as implied.

Why I Like SPYI

First of all, let us understand why I present SPYI as the representative covered call play in this comparison. This is because SPYI has done really well despite more promising challengers, such as GPIX. The GPIX versus SPYI was the backbone of my most recent SPYI Buy call, so I will start from there and see whether SPYI continues to command the Buy rating within its peer space, especially since the markets have stalled and even corrected a bit since then.

In that last thesis written in October last year, I had shown how SPYI has no business matching a more flexible strategy like GPIX on paper, yet it often holds up far better than expected. The gap narrows because what looks like a structural limitation in SPYI actually functions as a stabilizing feature across most market environments. GPIX’s variable coverage framework offers clear theoretical advantages, but those benefits depend on how effectively they are implemented. When positioning is not aligned with market moves, the intended edge can diminish quickly, as I have observed recently.

SPYI, by contrast, derives strength from a more consistent structure. Its call spread approach retains a portion of upside while still generating income, which reduces the need for precise adjustments. Additionally, SPYI's far higher allowed portfolio coverage (not capped explicitly at 75% like GPIX) becomes particularly relevant in range-bound markets, where more premium can be harvested.

Since October, in relatively less bullish conditions, I do not see much gap in total returns between SPYI and GPIX. In short, SPYI continues to defend its more popular footing as an all-weather, dependable covered call ETF (AUM over $8 billion versus GPIX's ~$3.3 billion)—simpler structure and more reliable, especially in range-bound markets. And, as the charts below show, it does trace the underlying index quite well for rebounds/rallies of 5-6% at a stretch (like in November 2025), so the upside capping fears kick in only when we are talking about significantly sharp rallies. There too, the opportunity lost should not be huge, as SPYI does implement a covered call spread structure, reopening rally capture after a higher strike.

What Does QDPL Do

That's where we park SPYI, a reliable income plan that does not really lag the S&P 500 unless we are talking about a sharp rally like say 10% within a short period of time (~4% rebound within a month has been captured well in November, as we can see, and slower rebounds until January 2026 have seen SPYI do better than even SPY in total return terms). It does punch above its weight in real-world performance. And that is why we bring SPYI as a covered call alternative to QDPL's dividend amplification strategy.

Now let's turn to the challenger, QDPL (AUM ~$1.5 billion), and understand what it does. QDPL is best viewed as an S&P exposure plus a leveraged bet on dividend expectations, not a simple high dividend strategy. The strategy allocates ~90% to the S&P 500 and adds a dividend component targeting ~400% of the index’s normal dividend exposure using exchange-traded dividend futures.

The S&P 500 exposure is implemented as physical stock holdings, and that accounts for an assured layer of dividends from it. That exposure can vary around the 90% mark depending on how much cash is required to implement the leveraged dividend futures position (or market conditions). The remaining capital is parked in Treasuries or income assets to act as collateral for the leveraged futures position (they add modest yield, often offsetting leverage and derivative costs).

What QDPL Needs to Work

In the case of QDPPL, the amplified dividend leg earns the difference between actual and expected dividends, not the full dividend itself. That means it needs a very narrow market condition to do better than the S&P 500—a condition where actual dividends beat expectations meaningfully. QDPL's performance has nothing to do with the absolute dividend regime. It could see drags even in high dividend regimes if that's already embedded in market expectations.

Even in favorable scenarios where actual dividends exceed expectations, the differential must be sufficiently wide. The leveraged dividend overlay needs to generate enough excess return to offset the drag from lower S&P 500 exposure before contributing positively to relative performance.

Take, for example, a case where the S&P 500 has rallied 5% over a period. Since QDPL has only ~90% exposure to this, it captures ~4% upside from the underlying portfolio. During this same period, it needs to not only see its dividend futures bet succeed (i.e., actual dividend turning out to be more than expected), but the dividend outperformance should be meaningful enough, such that amplification more than offsets its lower portfolio return, say effectively more than a 0.25% difference between the actual and expected dividends, so that a 4x amplification overrides the lower S&P 500 exposure. This is, of course, a simplistic illustrative example that does not account for the dividends from the stocks held or treasury yields and leverage costs. But it helps explain how QDPL needs to get the bet right and also by a certain magnitude to be able to outperform the S&P 500.

SPYI, on the other hand, monetizes volatility and time rather than forecasting accuracy. It does not require being right about future outcomes; it only needs markets that are not persistently trending sharply upward (sharper than ~4% a month, say). In other words, QDPL requires being directionally and quantitatively right on dividends, whereas SPYI appears to be a broader income strategy that performs across a wider range of outcomes.

Fixing the Market Variable

The wider applicability of SPYI and its higher odds of doing well versus QDPL across most market conditions (as discussed in the previous section) is not a mutually exclusive case, though. We could be in markets where both do well or both underperform — the performance criteria for each are independent of the other. And there are other nuances too, such as income and NAV repercussions. So, fixing the market conditions helps focus the thesis.

Now let's focus on the forward view. What we are already seeing is a slower and choppy market where SPYI should add definite value because it will earn income from premiums both if markets consolidate or correct further. It will keep up with slower upsides, and SPYI's recent performance shows us glimpses of this expectation already playing out.

The next few quarters are likely to see slower headline growth in the S&P 500. There is a mix of geopolitical uncertainty, rates staying higher for longer, and AI trades cooling after valuation extensions in 2024-25. The CapEx drag in hyperscalers is also capping upsides (and S&P 500 is dominated by growth tech with long-duration assumptions).

The tech promise and secular AI tailwinds are not broken, though. I fully expect markets to hold up and not see further severe drawdowns from here. The balanced view is net consolidation with volatility over the next few quarters, until the economics of the AI ecosystem (and how CapEx returns shape up) and AI impact in areas like software (under selling pressure today due to uncertainties) become clearer. A consolidating market is SPYI territory. SPYI's methodology of covered call spreads could add premium income here, and the NAV should also remain resilient.

It is important to understand that SPYI can track the S&P 500 relatively well if there is a slower rebound of up to 4-5% too (as we have seen in November 2025). In drawdowns, SPYI will add premium income and mitigate generally 2-3 percent of an S&P 500 fall. However, NAV erosion comes into play because of the dual impact of the underlying portfolio correcting and payouts exceeding the option income in this case.

As we have discussed before, a favorable market condition for SPYI (i.e., flat markets with volatility) does not necessarily mean underperformance in QDPL because it needs actual dividend outcomes to be higher than expectations, something that could happen irrespective of market conditions. However, the 2026 outlook is not only that of a headline index consolidation/pressure due to valuations, but one where earnings are slower than earlier. I have presented a detailed analysis showing slowing earnings upgrade momentum in the top S&P 500 components in another article. That may be more applicable for tech and less so for financials and utilities, but it is also true that non-tech sectors generally operate with more stable and predictable payout policies. Dividends are closely tied to earnings visibility, capital requirements, and regulatory frameworks. That leaves little room for positive surprises structurally, especially when earnings become slower due to higher than expected funding and operating costs in a sticky inflation and higher rates environment.

QDPL also needs dividend surprises, not stability. To that end, the SPY dividend growth has been slowing since the rebound from the 2020 and 2021-22 markets. That means, even if there are surprises, amplifying a smaller difference at best is unlikely to match the regime QDPL has been through in the recent past.

Long-Term Thesis - Beyond Current Markets

For the long-term thesis, one of the misleading graphs that often point favorably for QDPL is the total return charts since 2022-23, where we see QDPL do far better than SPYI in what has been a unidirectionally bullish market for the S&P 500. That often supports the thesis that even SPYI's theoretical option strategy implies — the thesis that SPYI is not for strongly bullish markets.

That is not entirely true. Historical charts can narrate different stories depending on the time period used. In this case, if you take out SPYI's lag as markets took off in 2024, you will see SPYI has not really suffered from its lack of ability to capture upside. The total return chart below is plotted from mid-2024 until date and shows how SPYI has been ahead of QDPL and almost tracked the S&P 500 in a strong bull market, especially the strong run from the April 2025 lows. If SPYI can debunk its bull market underperformance expectations in these strong markets, we are essentially talking about rare strong rebounds only (even sharper than April 2025 onwards) when SPYI could suffer. That's a lot rarer than what QDPL needs to underperform. The starting problem in 2023-24 is a one-off for SPYI, something not seen in more recent history.

Coming to QDPL, its outperformance versus the S&P 500 needs very rare market conditions to emerge. SPYI at least can and has outperformed in the slower markets since October last year. QDPL needs to get dividend expectations right and by a strong magnitude to be able to outperform the S&P 500. In fact, if the actual dividends are lower than expected, QDPL could be strongly penalized. That's a relevant and material problem in pressured markets (like, say, a recession) we have not seen since its inception.

While not a precise indicator of dividend expectations versus actual outcomes, a regime of strongly accelerating dividend growth can serve as a broad proxy for when QDPL is more likely to work. Periods where dividend growth meaningfully picks up tend to coincide with positive surprises relative to expectations, which is where the strategy’s leveraged positioning can add value. However, as the historical data suggests, such accelerations are not necessarily seen across regimes (refer to the dividend and growth chart for SPY in the previous section). In most environments, dividend growth remains steady and predictable, limiting the scope for meaningful outperformance (or limited leverage benefits if the difference is low).

QDPL is likely to struggle in periods where dividends either decline (or fall short of expectations), which could be rare, but markets where SPYI could continue to do well, at least in total return terms. Regimes like the 2008-09 financial crisis or even the softer phases around 2015-16 saw dividend growth stall or turn negative, which could have translated into unfavorable outcomes for QDPL's strategy dependent on positive surprises. These are not edge cases but part of normal market cycles. Such conditions have largely been absent in the post-2021 period since QDPL's inception, which coincides with its decent performance.

Keep Both, But Overweight SPYI

QDPL does have some advantages that should be highlighted (justifying the "strong" Hold rating). Firstly, more than 80% of the income in 2025 was classified as return of capital (ROC). That means deferred taxation and potential classification as capital gains eventually - more efficient than a primarily dividend income payout classified as ordinary income and taxed at income tax slabs. Overall, QDPL is reasonably tax-friendly. But so is SPYI with significant ROC. In March 2026, SPYI's payout had around 97% ROC. These distribution characteristics could vary across regimes for both the ETFs, but overall QDPL is not necessarily a more tax-efficient ETF.

The only real relative advantage in QDPL is its lower yield levels that preserve greater NAV, while SPYI's yields are higher and could be NAV erosive over time. Reinvestments are not efficient because of tax events introduced. But it is equally true that QDPL could also return capital if its income generation engine falters. Or it could see visibly lower yields, like it did in early 2025.

Overall, balancing the long-term thesis, short-term market outlook, and structural pros and cons, I feel that for the regime ahead, I would overweight SPYI in my income portfolio. The expected performance gap trumps tax and NAV erosion concerns. Hence, SPYI retains a Buy rating. QDPL is not necessarily going to perform worse, but it is not as versatile. I rate it as a strong Hold for an income portfolio riding the S&P 500.

data.title: Private Credit: Why It's Not 2008
data.description:
If you open the financial newspapers today, you get a sense of déjà vu. The thousands of stories about troubled funds offered by newspapers and news sites, blocked redemption requests, and uncollectable loans seem to have come straight from the headlines that preceded the subprime mortgage disaster. Everyone's screaming about the collapse of the system. I must say that even I was very superficially convinced of this. I, too, began to think that everything would soon collapse. Then I remembered that my job is precisely to understand what's behind things, analyze, and get to the bottom of the news, and I have all the tools to do so. I work in the sector professionally and have access to a lot of software and subscriptions that are expensive but offer so much added value.

So one day I stopped and said, "Are we really in a new 2008 like everyone is telling us?" Pause for a moment because betting on a sudden Lehman Brothers-style financial "heart attack" is the worst tactical mistake you can make today.

Before writing this article, I wasn't sure how to approach it. Many analysts and newspapers are talking about it, and I wanted to add value. I wanted to offer my perspective as well as report the facts objectively. So it took me time not only to gather and select the right information but also, and above all, to figure out how to interpret this analysis. Then I thought: if I were a reader of Seeking Alpha, what would I really want to read today? My answer was "an instruction manual." And here it is, your instruction manual to understand what's really happening and, above all, how to position your portfolio to avoid being crushed (and perhaps emerge a winner).

Heart Attack vs. Chronic Illness

The reason I'm bringing up a heart attack is that the markets literally suffered one. It was in 2008. Banks had toxic derivatives that were all tied to residential retail mortgages. These derivatives went bust. In the movie "Margin Call," you see the events of the last night. That is why we are discussing a heart attack.

We are not dealing with the same type of heart attack today. Instead, we are dealing with a chronic, long-term, painful disease. Unlike banks, private credit funds have the ability to contractually restrict investors from withdrawing their money using what are called "gates" (usually 5% per quarter repayment limitations). This is not the type of event that causes an immediate collapse on Wall Street. Instead, this is the type of event where thousands of small to medium-sized businesses will be zombified due to high interest rates and will not be able to grow.

Do you think you're out of it because you're not a millionaire? Wrong. Thanks to a regulatory change last year, private credit funds were included within pension plans (like 401ks), so (I'm sorry to tell you if you didn't know) unknowingly, the life savings of millions of people are sitting on what analysts call a gigantic "game of musical chairs." And when the music stops, there won't be enough seats for everyone. But don't be too scared; finish reading this article, and you'll understand better.

A Timeline of Disaster: How It All Went Down

Note to readers: At this point, I can assume that investors that follow the private credit market closely on a daily basis are well aware of what contributed to the current state of the market.

Below, I'll provide the chronology of events (I'll go back a bit in time), so if you all know how we got here, feel free to skip this section. Otherwise, make yourself comfortable; it might be an enjoyable (or perhaps not) re-reading of what happened.

I tried to highlight every aspect that relates to the problem of private credit, but if I've missed any steps, please let me know in the comments, adding more value to this analysis. Now, we can continue moving forward from here.

First, and as I said before, I'd like to point out that I'm going back a little further in time. I believe the real problems began a few years ago and that the effects are only now being seen. Let's start at the source.

2020 - 2023: The Golden Age

During the period of zero interest rates, private credit explodes. Middle-market firms (that were largely funded by private equity firms) were being given all sorts of free-flowing money. The loans had variable interest rates. At the time, this appeared to be an attractive option for investors because when central banks started raising interest rates violently in 2022–2023, the interest payments for these companies skyrocketed. However, formal default rates remained very low. So why didn't companies just default? The answer is simple. Private credit fund managers increasingly relied on mechanisms such as:

  • "Amend and Extend": A loan cannot be repaid as scheduled by a company. The fund manager will extend the time for the company to make repayment. As well, the fund manager may alter the original terms of the loan agreement; typically with an increased rate of interest. This way, the problem is put off until a better time.
  • "Payment-in-Kind" (PIK): When a company has insufficient funds to make the required payments to the lender, PIK is used. PIK means instead of requiring the lender to receive the required interest in cash, the lender receives the interest as additional principal to the original loan.

As a result, the issue was simply ignored.

2024: Problems in the fundamentals

With higher interest rates for longer, the debt burden became unaffordable for the real economy. In mid-2024, Standard & Poor's (S&P) and Fitch announced that they were observing many more private company ratings "downgrades" than ratings "updates." This was the first time the true liquidity mismatch of this market was recognized. Funds stated that they could allow investors to withdraw their money every quarter. However, capital was invested in illiquid loans with maturities between five and seven years.

2025: The First Crash and Repayment Stops

In 2025, things start to go wrong, and the financial illusion starts to crumble. Early in 2025, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) releases an alarming report that states that approximately 40% of companies that were financed by private credit are generating negative cash flow. This means those companies do not generate sufficient revenue to cover the costs of interest payments. In September, the first large-scale bankruptcies begin to occur (such as the bankruptcies of First Brands Group and Tricolor), and liquidity panic sets in. Because of increasing redemption requests, several leading firms in the industry restrict access to their funds (industry-wide "gating"), such as Cliffwater, which can only fulfill approximately 50% of redemption requests, and Morgan Stanley, which also imposes similar restrictions.

The liquidity crisis, or the run on "shadow credit" banks, is indeed happening. It is evident from the chart in the Financial Times that the number of redemption requests from investors seeking to withdraw from private credit funds (including Blue Owl and Morgan Stanley) has exceeded the warning threshold, and therefore, the fund managers have restricted all withdrawals.

Early 2026 (Today): The "SaaSpocalypse"

We are currently in a time when there is an extreme disruption of the markets caused by the combination of two very negative forces:

  • The AI Shock: Many private equity firms have made significant investments into B2B software companies, which had previously been viewed as being more stable than most other investment options because they generated revenue from subscription-based services. This all changed quickly with the emergence of the rapidly developing artificial intelligence industry. As such, many of the B2B software companies that private equity firms had invested in became obsolete overnight. Experts are calling the events taking place in the software sector today "The SaaSpocalypse" as they see loan values falling off a cliff in the software sector.

As you can see from this chart, most of the investment exposure in private credit portfolios was in B2B and IT software. Unfortunately, this was a huge mistake, given that the advent of AI caused a complete change in the way the software sector operates.

  • Rising Stress and Higher Default Activity: Blackstone’s enormous private credit fund (BCRED, $83 billion) recorded its first monthly loss in over three years in February. As a result of large-scale redemption requests, Blackstone used the personal funds of its top-level management team to repay fleeing investors. Furthermore, according to Fitch Ratings, the default rate in private credit has risen to 9.2%.

Therefore, I firmly believe that the primary concern today is that the collapse could lead to a "contagion". Traditional banks have extended over $250 billion in loans to these private credit funds. Therefore, if the funds collapse under the burden of defaults and redemption requests, the losses would be passed through directly to the bank balance sheet.

Credit Structuring And Rising Stress Signals

The major investment banks are not at all surprised by the present crisis. While they are giving you the impression that there is nothing to worry about, in reality they are working hard to create the instruments for their top hedge fund clients to short (bet against) the private credit market, which totals $1.8 trillion. There are some structural similarities with past credit cycles, particularly in the use of securitization and risk transfer mechanisms. It gets worse.

In today's market, the behemoths of the industry are finding themselves with hundreds of millions of dollars in their mutual fund portfolios in the form of "trouble loans" (the ones issued to the failed software companies mentioned above). Therefore, how do they protect their interests?

Possible methods include the use of CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations), where high-risk (or "stressed") loans are bundled into one product and sold on the market, spreading the credit risk among the various tranches. Investors may purchase the same portfolio of underlying assets with varying levels of credit risk. Theoretically, this aggregation of similar assets will improve both the comprehensive diversification and liquidity of the system. Nevertheless, it is not uncommon for this type of process to create "a veil" for the true credit quality of a loan, particularly at the end of a business cycle and/or with regard to more complicated financial products.

Musical Chairs & Natural Selection: my predictions

What will happen next? Aaron Brown, a Bloomberg analyst, said something perfectly fitting when he referred to the sector as now having simply started a ferocious game of musical chairs.

Debtors do not possess the funds needed to make payments. Institutional funds do not possess sufficient liquidity to repay their investors. And everybody is frantically attempting to collect each and every last cent available to them before the music stops.

Over the next 12-24 months we shall clearly observe 3 distinct characteristics.

#1 A panic develops in the secondary market.

Secondary market transactions have reached an astronomical level of $225B in 2025 (source: Bloomberg Terminal). This suggests a significant increase in liquidity-driven secondary market activity. Take a look at how the secondary market transaction volume exploded upward in 2025.

#2 The sharks will be waiting for the blood to run.

If you were thinking that the large institutional investors in distressed debt will immediately step forward to purchase the distressed loans to rescue the distressed loan market by purchasing these distressed loans, you are mistaken. They will not buy these distressed loan portfolios because many of the distressed loans being sold today are of very low quality. The real "sharks" of finance don't buy bad loans at the bottom of the first downturn but patiently wait for the funds to fail and then acquire those assets at a fraction of their original value. Another déjà vu?

#3 The stealthy erosion of retail portfolios

We will not hear about the implosion of the distressed loan market in the evening news because the "gates" (the withdrawal restrictions) will allow the hemorrhaging to slow. What we will instead witness is the silent eroding of retail portfolio values. Dividend payments from private funds will be reduced; the inflated valuations of assets will be slowly deflated; and the returns from your investments will be depleted due to excessive management fees while your investment capital will remain tied up.

Market Implications: Winners, Losers, and What Could Go Right

As an analyst, it’s my responsibility to highlight that risks are building and deserve close attention from investors, but most importantly, it’s my goal to tell you where to invest your money. If you don’t take proactive steps when disasters occur, you’re leaving yourself open to missing a huge investment opportunity. That’s exactly what I am doing with my current investments. This is how I am structuring my portfolio currently.

The "Black List" (The things I'll never invest in)

I will not get into any unlisted or semi-liquid private credit funds. Those fund managers (and we've seen this with Blackstone's BCRED and Blue Owl), as they fight for the valuation of their hard-to-value assets, have the power to freeze your money in place when you try to withdraw.

I'm still concerned with many different types of investment that are extremely high in leverage and require continuous financing. The small-cap sector of the U.S. equity markets fits into this category. In addition to being subject to higher debt servicing costs as interest rates rise, these companies can be severely impacted by deteriorating financial conditions. As such, I have no interest in investing in the Russell 2000 Index. When a business is small, it usually gets crushed the most during an economic downturn and therefore a debt-crushing event.
